steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student claims ego-based (body-relative) conceptualization of time is universal — meaning all cultures do it. To weaken this, we need a finding showing at least one culture whose temporal reference frame is not body-relative. The Yupno are the studied group, so the finding must show Yupno speakers using a non-egocentric system.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘A Yupno speaker points in opposite directions when indicating a past event versus a future event.’: Pointing in opposite directions is consistent with ego-based spatial reference (e.g., back = past, front = future). This does not rule out an egocentric system, so it does not weaken the claim. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Some Yupno grammatical structures used when talking about time are also used in Spanish.’: Grammar overlap says nothing about whether either group uses ego-based spatial conceptualization. Irrelevant to the claim. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘When Yupno speakers who are outdoors use gestures to refer to the future, they point uphill from their current location regardless of which way they are facing.’: "Regardless of which way they are facing" is the key phrase. Egocentric systems change with body orientation. Pointing uphill no matter which way you face means the reference frame is fixed to the landscape — a non-egocentric system. This directly contradicts the claim that ego-based time is universal. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘Although Yupno speakers and Spanish speakers both use gestures to indicate orientation in time, Yupno speakers tend to use fewer gestures overall when speaking than Spanish speakers do.’: Both groups still use spatial gestures for time, which is consistent with (not against) ego-based conceptualization. Gesture frequency is unrelated to the type of reference frame used. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
